description: We were standing about 5 feet away from the willows at Pelican Point scanning for gulls. Something feeding low in the willows caught my eye and I turned around to get my binoculars on it. Immediately I noticed the orange hues on the face and the gray under the eye. I alerted Mitchel and Kathy to turn around very slowly as to not flush the bird. At that point I was not sure what it was but knew it was important to get photos and document. In my head I was running through possible sparrows and landed on either LCSP or NESP. It continued to feed low in the willows and would hop out onto the mudflat and on the ground in the willows as it fed. We sat down as it continued this behavior and took plenty of photos. Key identification marks noted in the field and after review of photos were: Structure: Small size about the size of a LISP, short tailed, front heavy appearancePlumage: Head; Crown with black strips and gray medial crown stripe, ending at clean gray nape. Orange extremely noticable above eye, no marking in superlores or line in front of eye, black eye line extending behind the eye ending at clean gray nape. Below the eye is a clean gray area lined by orange. Throat clean light orange. Body: orange in breast and extending down the flanks all the way to the undertail, clean white belly. Nape mentioned but clean gray. Back with 4 noticable white stripes on. Wings: no wing bars or noticable markings on wings, rufous in color. Tail: some orange/rufous noticable in tail but not prominent.
